title: Cheese scones
subtitle: Cheddar-sharp savoury scones
excerpt: Savoury scones built on the same quick method as the sweet version: cold butter rubbed into flour, brought together with cold milk and a generous amount of mature cheddar. Sharp, golden, and best eaten warm.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the standard scone method. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): the cheddar cheese scone as a specifically British savoury bake, distinct from the sweet afternoon-tea version.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Reheat from frozen in a 180°C oven for 8 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. Two trays on two shelves; swap halfway through baking.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Rub in the butter the night before and refrigerate. Add cheese and milk the next morning.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dough that has just come together with visible rough patches and uneven texture. For scones, this is correct — over-mixing produces a tight, dense result. The dough hydrates and smooths slightly during resting and in the oven.

content.text: The cheese scone exists to be eaten warm, ideally with butter. The cheese should be mature cheddar — sharp, with enough acid to cut through the butter fat in the dough. Mild cheddar disappears into the background. Use at least 140 g of cheese and grate it coarsely so there are visible threads in the crumb and on the top.

attrs.prepNote: coarsely grated, plus extra for the top

content.text: Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper.

Put the {{self-raising-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{fine-sea-salt}}, and {{mustard-powder}} in a large bowl. Add the cold {{unsalted-butter}} and rub it in with your fingertips until the mixture looks like rough breadcrumbs. Work quickly so the butter stays cold. Add 100 g of the grated {{mature-cheddar}} and toss it through the crumbs.
Pour in the cold {{whole-milk}} and mix quickly with a palette knife until a
forms. Do not knead or squeeze. If the dough looks too dry, add another tablespoon of milk.

Turn the dough onto a floured surface and pat it to 2.5 cm thick. Cut rounds with a 5 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Arrange on the tray, brush the tops with a little milk, and scatter over the remaining 40 g of grated cheese.

Bake for 12–15 minutes until the tops are deep golden and the cheese is bubbling and browned in patches. The scones should have risen with a slight crack around the equator. Eat warm, split with butter.
